title: Dart FFI — Calling C from Dart
objectives: Understand Dart FFI via a real-life analogy
See when FFI beats platform channels
Call C functions from Dart end-to-end

sections.markdown:
**Think of two rooms with different languages.**

- **Platform channels** are like passing a hand-written note under the door in an envelope. Each note gets serialized (put in an envelope), passed through the door, deserialized (opened), acted on, and a reply comes back the same way. It works — but there's overhead per note.
- **FFI (Foreign Function Interface)** is like installing a **speaking-tube directly between the two rooms**. You call the C function, execution jumps into C, does its work, jumps back. **No serialization. No channel hop. No async boundary.** For a computation, it's ~100x faster than a platform-channel round-trip.
- **When?** Native code you want to call millions of times a second: cryptography, image processing, database engines, ML inference, custom binary parsers. Any time overhead-per-call matters, FFI wins.

**Why this matters:** senior Flutter interviews love this. It's the escape hatch that connects Dart to the entire universe of C/C++/Rust libraries. Isar, sqflite, and many high-performance Flutter packages use FFI internally.

**Dart FFI** (Foreign Function Interface, `dart:ffi`) lets Dart code call native C functions directly, without serialization or platform channels. You load a dynamic library (`.so` / `.dylib` / `.dll`), look up function pointers by name, and invoke them with typed arguments — as if they were Dart functions. **Trade-off:** you manage memory manually, native crashes take down your app, and you must ship the compiled library for every target platform.
## FFI vs Platform Channels — when to pick each

rows: Battery, biometrics, camera, sensors, native SDKs
Crypto, image processing, DB engine, ML, hot compute loops

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — call C `add(int, int)` from Dart
## Working with strings and bytes — the `ffi` package helpers
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Forgetting to free allocations.** `calloc<Int32>()` gives you a pointer; you MUST `calloc.free(ptr)`. Wrap in `try/finally`. Memory leaks in native land are exactly as bad as anywhere else.

**Mistake 2: Passing Dart heap pointers into C.** Dart GC can move objects. If you pass a Dart-allocated string's underlying pointer into C, it may be GC'd or moved mid-call. Always convert to native memory via `.toNativeUtf8()` or explicit `calloc`.

**Mistake 3: Type mismatches.** `Int32` on the C side ≠ `Int64` on the Dart side. A signed/unsigned mismatch or wrong size will produce garbage or crash. Get the type mapping right; use the `ffigen` tool to generate bindings from C headers.
**Mistake 4: Native crashes = app crash.** A segfault in C is a `SIGSEGV` that takes down the whole Flutter process. No graceful handling. Design defensively; validate inputs; test on all platforms.
**Mistake 5: Blocking the UI thread on a slow FFI call.** FFI calls are SYNCHRONOUS. A 500 ms C function called from the UI thread = 500 ms of jank. Use `Isolate.run` for long native calls.
## Best practices
**1. Use `ffigen` to generate bindings from C headers.** Eliminates typos and type mismatches; regenerate when the header changes.
**2. Wrap allocations in `try/finally`.** Every `calloc<T>()` needs a `calloc.free(ptr)`.
**3. Isolate long-running FFI calls.** Wrap with `Isolate.run(() => nativeHeavyWork(x))` to keep the UI thread free.
**4. Test on every target platform.** Native library ABI differs between iOS/Android/macOS/Linux/Windows; CI matrices help.
**5. Use `NativeCallable` for C → Dart callbacks.** Modern async-safe way to receive callbacks from C code.
**6. Consider Rust for new native code.** Cleaner memory semantics than raw C; `flutter_rust_bridge` automates FFI generation.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — `add(int, int)`.** Build the sample. Compile a `.dylib` (macOS) or `.so` (Linux), call from Dart.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — String round-trip.** C function that receives a UTF-8 string and returns an uppercased copy.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — `ffigen`.** Point `ffigen` at a real C header; auto-generate the Dart bindings.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — SHA-256 hasher.** Call a C-crypto library from Dart; compare speed vs a pure-Dart implementation.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Ship a plugin.** Package a native library across Android + iOS + macOS + Linux + Windows so a Dart consumer just does `import`.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Dart FFI and when do you use it?**

**A:** Dart FFI (`dart:ffi`) is the Foreign Function Interface — a way for Dart to call C functions directly without serialization or async channels. You load a native shared library, look up function pointers by name, and invoke them with typed arguments. **Use it for:** high-throughput native work — cryptography, image processing, database engines (Isar, sqflite use it), ML inference, custom binary parsers, hot compute loops. **Don't use it for:** OS-native APIs (use platform channels for those). The core benefit is speed: an FFI call is ~microseconds vs ~milliseconds for a platform-channel round-trip.

**Q2: FFI vs Platform Channels — what's the difference?**

**A:** Different tools for different jobs. **Platform channels** — Dart ↔ Kotlin/Swift/Obj-C. Async. Message-based; every call is serialized/deserialized. Great for OS APIs (camera, sensors, biometrics). **FFI** — Dart ↔ C/C++/Rust. **Synchronous.** No serialization; direct function call. Great for high-throughput compute. **Cost picture:** platform channels ~1 ms per call (serialization dominates); FFI ~10 µs per call (function call overhead only). **Rule:** if you're calling something a million times, pick FFI. If you're calling the OS occasionally, pick channels. Both can coexist in one app.

**Q3: How do you handle memory with FFI?**

**A:** **Manually.** You allocate with `calloc<Uint8>(size)` (from the `ffi` package), get a `Pointer<T>`, use it, then `calloc.free(ptr)` when done. Wrap in `try/finally` to guarantee cleanup. For strings, `.toNativeUtf8()` allocates a UTF-8 buffer YOU must free; `.toDartString()` copies to a Dart String (auto-managed). **Gotchas:** (1) Dart-allocated memory is subject to GC — never pass it to C without converting via `.toNativeUtf8()` or `calloc`. (2) Match allocator/deallocator — if C uses `malloc`, you need `free` (not `calloc.free` from Dart if they differ). (3) `NativeFinalizer` (Dart 2.19+) can auto-free when the wrapping Dart object is GC'd.

**Q4: What is `ffigen` and why use it?**

**A:** `ffigen` (`package:ffigen`) is a code generator that reads a C header file and generates the corresponding Dart FFI bindings — typedefs, `NativeFunction` classes, `lookup` calls, memory type conversions. **Why:** hand-writing bindings is error-prone (typos, wrong type mappings between C's `int32_t` and Dart's `Int32` vs `Int64`). `ffigen` reads the ground-truth header and never gets confused. Regenerate when the C API changes; catch drift at compile time. **Every serious FFI-backed package uses `ffigen`** — hand-rolled bindings are a maintenance nightmare.

**Q5: How do you avoid blocking the UI thread with a slow FFI call?**

**A:** FFI calls are **synchronous** — a 500 ms C function called from the UI isolate freezes the UI for 500 ms. Fix: wrap the native call in an isolate. Modern: `await Isolate.run(() => NativeMath.heavyCompute(x))`. Isolate.run spawns a fresh isolate, runs the FFI call there, and returns the result — the UI isolate is never blocked. **Caveats:** the isolate boundary has a cost (~1-5 ms setup). For a call taking <10 ms, isolate overhead may dominate; don't isolate everything blindly. For 100 ms+ calls, always isolate.

**Q6: What are the risks of using FFI?**

**A:** **(1) Native crashes** — a segfault in C = SIGSEGV = **whole app crashes**. No graceful handling. **(2) Memory leaks** — no GC on the native side; you must manually free. **(3) Type mismatches** — a `int32_t` on the C side that Dart sees as `Int64` produces garbage. **(4) ABI differences** — Android arm64 vs Android x86_64 need separate builds; iOS's calling convention differs from Windows. **(5) Shipping complexity** — you must compile + ship the native library for every target. **(6) Debugging** — Dart's debugger doesn't step into C; you need lldb/gdb. **Rule:** these risks are real. Only use FFI when the perf gain justifies them. For most app work, platform channels are safer.

**Q7: Which popular Flutter packages use FFI?**

**A:** **`isar`** — a fast local DB written in Rust, exposed via FFI. **`sqflite_common_ffi`** — SQLite via FFI (used on desktop where the plugin-based one doesn't work). **`objectbox`** — another local DB, FFI-backed. **`crypto` libraries** — some cryptography implementations for perf. **`flutter_rust_bridge`** — meta-tool that generates FFI bindings between Dart and Rust. **`llama_cpp_dart`** — on-device LLM inference via FFI to llama.cpp. **The pattern:** any high-perf package eventually reaches for FFI. Understanding it well means you can (a) use those packages competently and (b) build your own when needed.

## Real-world usage

- **`isar`** — Rust NoSQL database exposed via FFI.
- **`sqflite` on desktop** — SQLite via FFI.
- **`llama.cpp` bindings** — on-device LLM inference.
- **`flutter_rust_bridge`** — Rust interop for Flutter.
- **Custom native SDKs** — payment processors, tracking libs, biometric libs shipped as C.
- **Games / graphics** — game logic in C, drawn via Flutter.
- **Cross-platform CLI tools** — Dart FFI to reuse a C lib on desktop.

## Advanced notes

- **`NativeCallable.listener` / `.isolateLocal`** — pass a Dart callback INTO C so it can call back to Dart. `.listener` is async-safe (callable from any thread).
- **`Struct` and `Union`** — declare C-compatible structs/unions in Dart.
- **`Array<T>` fields on Struct** — inline C arrays.
- **`NativeFinalizer`** — auto-free native memory when the Dart wrapper is GC'd.
- **`ffigen`** — code generator; supports typedefs, enums, structs, functions.
- **`Isolate.run` + FFI** — the modern way to offload native work.
- **iOS static linking** — combine your C into the Runner app (`DynamicLibrary.process()`).
- **Android CMake** — Android Studio has NDK support; add a `CMakeLists.txt` and Gradle builds the `.so`.
- **`Pointer.cast<T>()`** — reinterpret pointer types.
- **`Allocator`** — the interface behind `calloc`; you can implement your own for tracing/pooling.

flashcards.back: Direct native function calls from Dart to C/C++/Rust — no serialization, synchronous, microsecond latency.
FFI = Dart↔C, sync, µs. Channels = Dart↔Kotlin/Swift, async, ms. FFI for compute; channels for OS APIs.
Manual: calloc<T>(...) + calloc.free(ptr). Wrap in try/finally.
Use `ffigen` to generate from C headers — no typos.
Wrap with Isolate.run(() => heavyCall(x)); UI isolate stays free.
Native crashes = whole app crash; memory leaks; type-mismatches; per-platform ABI shipping.

challenges.text: Compile a C `add(int,int)` .dylib/.so; call it from Dart.
Round-trip a UTF-8 string: Dart → C → uppercase → back to Dart.
Generate FFI bindings from a real C header with `ffigen`.
Wrap a heavy C SHA-256 call with `Isolate.run` and compare timings vs pure Dart.
Use `NativeCallable.listener` to receive an async callback from C code back into Dart.
Package a native library into a Flutter plugin usable on Android + iOS + macOS + Linux + Windows.
